source.title: A neural signature of pattern separation in the monkey hippocampus
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

confidence_rationale: Single-unit recordings in non-human primate CA3/DG during a visual pattern separation task directly homologous to disambiguation. Primate data improves transfer to REE human-adjacent models. Mapping is indirect: REE's hippocampal rollout module must disambiguate z_world trajectories, but the paper measures neural responses to lure images rather than trajectory disambiguation per se. Confidence bounded by this abstraction gap.
failure_signatures: CA3/DG responses to lure images overlap substantially with repeat responses in some neurons, indicating pattern separation is probabilistic, not absolute.
A subpopulation of CA3/DG neurons drives the key discrimination; the majority are non-selective, implying sparse coding with high variance across trials.

mapping.source_claim_statement: When monkeys discriminate recently seen (repeat) from similar (lure) images in a pattern separation task, CA3/DG neurons respond to lures more like novel than repeat images; a subpopulation of these neurons classifies novel, lure, and repeat images from neural firing rates.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-018 requires the hippocampal rollout module to construct trajectory proposals that are genuinely distinct from previously traversed paths even when the z_world states are highly similar. This paper provides direct primate evidence that CA3/DG is the anatomical locus performing this disambiguation -- supporting the claim that REE's hippocampal module needs a CA3/DG-equivalent pattern separation mechanism for non-redundant rollout generation. Motivates new claim MECH-147: DG-mediated pattern separation gates trajectory disambiguation in hippocampal rollout.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper measures item-level disambiguation (visual images), not trajectory-level disambiguation (action sequences in a residue field). The transfer from image-lure discrimination to trajectory proposal disambiguation in z_world is a significant abstraction step. Pattern separation may operate at different time scales in trajectory-domain tasks.
mapping.source_context: Primate (Macaca mulatta), visual recognition, single-unit electrophysiology, CA3/DG, comparison of repeat vs lure image responses

confidence_components.notes: High venue (PNAS), primate data, clean experimental design. Mapping fidelity moderate: image lure discrimination != trajectory disambiguation, but the core mechanism (CA3/DG resolving overlapping inputs into distinct output codes) is directly analogous to the REE rollout disambiguation requirement.
